title: PA State Rep.ValerieGaydosServing Pennsylvania's 44th Legislative District
raw_text:
Jun
17
Press Release
Gaydos: Impact Fee Revenue Supports Local Priorities
Read More

ai_summary.summary: Legislator Gaydos issued a statement about impact fee revenue being distributed to local communities in Pennsylvania. Impact fees are charges collected from natural gas drillers that are then allocated to municipalities and counties for local projects and priorities. The release highlights how these funds support local government needs.

ai_summary.summary: This is a listing of press releases from the Pennsylvania Department of Revenue, not a single legislator release. The releases cover routine administrative actions including monthly tax collection reports, the Property Tax/Rent Rebate Program application cycles, a new Working Pennsylvanians Tax Credit, and various taxpayer assistance initiatives spanning late 2024 through mid-2026. No single legislative action is being described.

title: Gaydos: Impact Fee Revenue Supports Local Priorities
raw_text:
Gaydos: Impact Fee Revenue Supports Local Priorities
June 17, 2026
HARRISBURG - Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) today highlighted nearly $251,000 in Marcellus Shale impact fee revenue headed to municipalities across the 44th Legislative District, along with more than $2.04 million for Allegheny County. The funding gives local governments added flexibility to address infrastructure needs, public services and other community priorities.
вАЬPennsylvaniaвАЩs energy industry continues to provide meaningful benefits to our communities,вАЭ said Gaydos. вАЬThese impact fee dollars help local governments address important needs such as road maintenance, public infrastructure, environmental stewardship and public safety services. The funds also help to ease the burden on local taxpayers.вАЭ
The following impact fee disbursements in the 44th District were announced:
вАҐ	Aleppo Township - $1,041.
вАҐ	Bell Acres Borough - $1,683.
вАҐ	Crescent Township - $1,851.
вАҐ	Edgeworth Borough - $1,537.
вАҐ	Findlay Township - $190,057.
вАҐ	Glen Osborne Borough - $519.
вАҐ	Glenfield Borough - $86.
вАҐ	Haysville Borough - $30.
вАҐ	Leet Township - $1,065.
вАҐ	Leetsdale Borough - $1,152.
вАҐ	Moon Township - $17,313.
вАҐ	North Fayette Township - $29,942.
вАҐ	Sewickley Borough - $2,839.
вАҐ	Sewickley Heights Borough - $939.
вАҐ	Sewickley Hills Borough - $493.
For the reporting year of 2025, natural gas producers paid almost $244 million in impact fees statewide, for a total of more than $3.12 billion since the program began in 2012.
Under Act 13 of 2012, impact fees are collected from drilling companies, with a significant portion returned directly to counties and municipalities affected by natural gas development. The remaining revenue supports the Marcellus Shale Legacy Fund and statewide initiatives, including environmental programs, emergency response planning and training, water and sewer infrastructure projects and transportation improvements.
Representative Valerie Gaydos
44th Legislative District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives
Media Contact: Rick Leiner
717.260.6437
rleiner@pahousegop.com
RepGaydos.com / Facebook.com/RepGaydos
Share

ai_summary.summary: Rep. Valerie Gaydos announced that municipalities in her district will receive approximately $251,000 in Marcellus Shale natural gas impact fee revenue, with Allegheny County receiving an additional $2.04 million. These funds are automatically distributed under a 2012 state law (Act 13) that requires drilling companies to pay fees, a portion of which goes to local governments. Gaydos did not sponsor legislation or take a direct action; she is publicizing a routine annual disbursement.

title: Gaydos Supports Property Tax Relief for Seniors, Funding for Pennsylvania Schools
raw_text:
Gaydos Supports Property Tax Relief for Seniors, Funding for Pennsylvania Schools
June 10, 2026
HARRISBURG - Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) voted in favor of
House Bill 1678
, legislation that would provide additional property tax rebates to Pennsylvania homeowners while ensuring funding reaches all 500 school districts across the Commonwealth.
The legislation would amend Pennsylvania's Tax Reform Code to generate funding for enhanced property tax relief, with a focus on helping seniors and other homeowners facing rising costs. It also updates provisions related to the stateвАЩs gross receipts tax to support the property tax rebate program.
вАЬMany seniors are living on fixed incomes and feeling the impact of rising costs every day,вАЭ said Gaydos. вАЬAdditional property tax relief can help them remain in their homes and communities. This legislation is a starting point because it provides relief for homeowners while ensuring all 500 school districts across Pennsylvania receive funding. We do not have to choose between supporting taxpayers and supporting our schools. We can do both.вАЭ
House Bill 1678 passed the House with bipartisan support and now heads to the Senate for further consideration.
Representative Valerie Gaydos
44th Legislative District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives
Media Contact: Rick Leiner
717.260.6437
rleiner@pahousegop.com
RepGaydos.com / Facebook.com/RepGaydos

title: Gaydos Supports Measure to Reduce PFAS Exposure in Everyday Consumer Products
raw_text:
Gaydos Supports Measure to Reduce PFAS Exposure in Everyday Consumer Products
June 10, 2026
HARRISBURG - Rep. Valerie Gaydos (R-Allegheny) today announced her support for legislation that would prohibit the use of harmful per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) chemicals in a variety of high-contact consumer products commonly used by Pennsylvanians.
House Bill 2145
, which passed the House with bipartisan support, would ban the intentional use of PFAS in products such as cosmetics, dental floss, menstrual products and certain infant and children's items.
вАЬI was pleased to co-sponsor this legislation and vote in favor of it because protecting the health and safety of Pennsylvania families should always be a priority,вАЭ said Gaydos. вАЬMany of these products are used every day by families who may not realize they are being exposed to potentially harmful chemicals. This commonsense measure takes an important step toward reducing that exposure while giving manufacturers time to adapt.вАЭ
PFAS are a class of synthetic chemicals that have been linked to serious health concerns, including cancer, immune system suppression, fertility issues, pregnancy-related complications and other adverse health effects. Often referred to as вАЬforever chemicals,вАЭ PFAS do not readily break down in the environment and can accumulate in soil, water and the human body over time.
Research has shown that PFAS-containing consumer products can contribute to environmental contamination, including groundwater and drinking water supplies, when discarded in landfills. Children may be especially vulnerable to exposure due to their frequent contact with products that can contain these chemicals.
The legislation would give manufacturers time to transition to safer alternatives while reducing the health and environmental risks associated with PFAS.
House Bill 2145 now advances to the Pennsylvania Senate for consideration.
Representative Valerie Gaydos
44th Legislative District
Pennsylvania House of Representatives
Media Contact: Rick Leiner
717.260.6437
rleiner@pahousegop.com
RepGaydos.com / Facebook.com/RepGaydos

The bill now moves to the Pennsylvania Senate for consideration. PFAS are synthetic chemicals linked to cancer and other health risks that persist in the environment and human body.
